title: Student Welfare: Crisis Intervention
description: Conroe ISD's FFB policy governs how the district responds to student crises, including threats of violence and suicide risk. It establishes the structure and qualifications of threat assessment teams, outlines procedures for conducting assessments, and addresses parental notification and consent requirements. The policy also includes provisions related to mental health services, special education students, substance use, and record retention.

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's FFB (Crisis Intervention) Local Policy substantially expands and restructures the threat assessment framework, adding provisions for multi-campus teams, parental notification before assessments, written parental consent for mental health services, special education student protections, record retention requirements, and clarifying training provider language, while modernizing terminology throughout (e.g., replacing "substance abuse program" with "substance use prevention and intervention").
changes_added: A team may serve more than one campus of the District, provided that: 1. Each District campus is assigned a team; and 2. In serving a particular campus, the team includes the person designated to serve as the campus behavior coordinator for that campus.
this Policy, and the District multi-hazard operational plan (added to list of team expertise areas)
The Superintendent shall adopt procedures to implement this Policy and Texas law, consistent with the model policies and procedures developed by the Texas School Safety Center.

Notice of a Threat section: The Superintendent shall require that, as soon as safe and practicable after an administrator or team for a District campus receives information regarding a threat made against that campus, including through social media, the administrator or team immediately provide to each member of the teaching staff, including teacher's aides, who may be directly affected by the threat notice that includes: (1) a statement of the existence of the threat; (2) the nature of the threat; and (3) any other pertinent details to ensure student and staff safety.
the Texas School Safety Center or a regional education service center regarding evidence-based threat assessment programs.
to the team (in Employee Confidentiality section)

for the team to assess and report individuals who make threats of violence or exhibit harmful, threatening, or violent behavior, gathering and analyzing data to determine the level of risk and appropriate intervention, including: 1. referring a student for mental health assessment; and 2. implementing an escalation procedure, if appropriate based on the team's assessment, in accordance with District policy.
making (replacing prior word in threat assessment process step 1)
the team or designee will (in step 2 pre-assessment language)
Notify (replacing 'Notifying' — grammatical correction to match 'will')
Provide (replacing 'Provideing' — grammatical correction, step 2b)
Provide (replacing 'Provideing' — grammatical correction, step 2c)

If a student in a special education program is the subject of a threat assessment, the team conducting the threat assessment must include at least one of the following persons who has specific knowledge of the student's disability and the disability's manifestations: (1) a special education teacher who provides instruction to the student; (2) a behavior analyst licensed under Chapter 506, Occupations Code; (3) a clinical or master social worker licensed under Chapter 505, Occupations Code; or (4) a specialist in school psychology licensed under Chapter 501, Occupations Code. The team may also include: (1) an educational diagnostician; (2) a behavior specialist.
if the individual is a student (qualifying language in serious-risk-of-violence reporting requirement)
This requirement does not prevent an employee of the school from acting immediately to prevent an imminent threat or respond to an emergency.

A team may not provide a mental health care service to a student who is under 18 years of age unless the team obtains written consent from the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student before providing the mental health care service. The consent required must be submitted on a form developed by the District that complies with all applicable state and federal law. The student's parent or person standing in parental relation to the student may give consent for a student to receive ongoing services or may limit consent to one or more services provided on a single occasion.

Materials and information provided to or produced by a team during a threat assessment of a student under this section must be maintained in the student's school record until the student's 24th birthday.

Additionally, the Superintendent shall coordinate with law enforcement authorities as necessary and take other appropriate action in accordance with the District's multi-hazard emergency operations plan.
procedures related to substance use prevention and intervention.
to the community, school, or individual and support the District in implementing the District's multihazard emergency operations plan
changes_removed: an approved provider on evidence-based threat assessment programs.
to another person, the campus, or the community
timeline_local.notes: The cover page title heading is not a traditional action title (e.g., "Discuss Revisions" or "Adopt Revisions") — it reads as a policy document header rather than a board agenda action item, so action is marked 'unknown'. The redline is Version 1, suggesting this is a new local policy being established. Red underlined and red strikethrough formatting was clearly distinguishable. Several additions involve grammatical corrections (Notifying→Notify, Provideing→Provide) that appear as tracked changes. The Notice of a Threat section header and its full text are entirely red-underlined, indicating it is a new section addition.

elements.element: Multi-campus team structure: a team may serve more than one campus, provided each campus is assigned a team and the campus behavior coordinator is included
elements.supporting_quote: A team may serve more than one campus of a school district, provided that each district campus is assigned a team. | each district campus is assigned a team.
elements.local_additions: The statute permits multi-campus teams (Permitted by Law) and requires each campus be assigned a team (Required by Law). The specific requirement that when serving a particular campus the team must include that campus's designated campus behavior coordinator is a local addition beyond the statute's express text — Locally Discretionary. The overall element is Mixed across all three sub-classifications.

elements.element: Addition of 'this Policy, and the District multi-hazard operational plan' to team expertise areas
elements.local_additions: TEC §37.115 enumerates specific expertise areas (counseling, behavior management, mental health and substance use, classroom instruction, special education, school administration, school safety and security, emergency management, and law enforcement). Appending 'this Policy and the District multi-hazard operational plan' as additional expertise areas is a district choice with no matching statutory requirement or permission.

elements.element: Superintendent shall adopt procedures consistent with Texas School Safety Center model policies
elements.supporting_quote: The board of trustees of each school district shall establish a threat assessment and safe and supportive school team... and policies and procedures for the teams that are consistent with the model policies and procedures developed by the Texas School Safety Center.
elements.verification_method: verbatim statute text | web search confirmed
elements.local_additions: The policy assigns this obligation specifically to the Superintendent rather than the board, which is a permissible local delegation choice, but the core obligation to adopt TxSSC-consistent procedures is statutory.

elements.element: Notice of a Threat section: Superintendent shall require that, as soon as safe and practicable, administrators or team immediately provide teaching staff (including aides) who may be directly affected with notice of the threat's existence, nature, and pertinent details
elements.verification_method: web search confirmed — no matching verbatim statutory text identified in TEC §37.115 or any other cited statute in the statute analysis; no enacted provision specifically mandating this staff notification protocol was confirmed
elements.local_additions: This entire provision — the Superintendent mandate, 'as soon as safe and practicable' timing standard, scope to teaching staff and aides, and three-part content requirement (existence, nature, pertinent details) — is a district-designed framework. No verbatim statute quote in the provided analysis supports this specific staff notification requirement. Under the Grounding Rule, this element classifies as Locally Discretionary.

elements.element: Training provider language: Texas School Safety Center or a regional education service center regarding evidence-based threat assessment programs (replacing 'an approved provider')
elements.supporting_quote: each team to complete training provided by the TxSSC or a regional education service center (ESC)
elements.verification_method: verbatim statute text | web search confirmed
elements.local_additions: None — the policy update aligns policy language with the statutory provider list, removing the prior vague 'approved provider' language.

elements.element: Employee Confidentiality: limiting disclosure 'to the team' (addition of qualifying language in confidentiality section)
elements.local_additions: The statute analysis contains no verbatim quote requiring or expressly authorizing this specific qualifying language in the employee confidentiality provision. This is a local drafting clarification.

elements.element: Team's core functions: assessing and reporting individuals who make threats or exhibit harmful/threatening/violent behavior; gathering and analyzing data to determine risk and appropriate intervention, including referring a student for mental health assessment and implementing an escalation procedure
elements.supporting_quote: assessing and reporting individuals who make threats of violence or exhibit harmful, threatening, or violent behavior | gathering and analyzing data to determine the level of risk and appropriate intervention, including: referring a student for mental health assessment; and implementing an escalation procedure, if appropriate
elements.local_additions: None — the policy language tracks the statutory mandate closely.

elements.element: Grammatical corrections ('making,' 'the team or designee will,' 'Notify,' 'Provide' x2) in threat assessment process steps
elements.local_additions: These are purely editorial and grammatical corrections to pre-existing locally drafted procedural language. No statute requires or dictates this specific wording.

elements.element: Special education student protections: team conducting a threat assessment of a special education student must include at least one qualified professional with specific knowledge of the student's disability (special ed teacher, licensed behavior analyst, licensed clinical/master social worker, or licensed specialist in school psychology); team may also include an educational diagnostician or behavior specialist
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §37.115(d-1), as added by H.B. 6 (89th Leg., R.S., 2025)
elements.supporting_quote: Requires a threat assessment team and safe and supportive school conducting the threat assessment under Subsection (f), notwithstanding Subsection (d), if a student in a special education program under Subchapter A, Chapter 29, is the subject of the threat assessment, to include at least one of certain professionals who has specific knowledge of the student's disability and the disability's manifestations.
elements.verification_method: web search confirmed — H.B. 6 (89th Leg.) enacted and effective June 20, 2025; TEC §37.115(d-1) added
elements.local_additions: The mandatory inclusion of at least one qualifying professional is Required by Law. The 'team may also include' list (educational diagnostician, behavior specialist) mirrors the statute's permissive language and is Permitted by Law. The combination makes this element Mixed.

elements.element: Qualifying language 'if the individual is a student' in serious-risk-of-violence reporting requirement
elements.local_additions: The statute analysis provides no verbatim quote requiring or expressly authorizing this specific qualifying clause. This is a locally drafted clarification of scope.

elements.element: 'This requirement does not prevent an employee of the school from acting immediately to prevent an imminent threat or respond to an emergency' (exception to pre-assessment parental notification requirement)
elements.verification_method: verbatim statute text — Grounding Rule applied; no verbatim quote in the statute analysis for H.B. 473 / TEC §37.115 creates this imminent-threat exception
elements.local_additions: While this exception is a reasonable policy interpretation of general emergency-powers principles, no supporting verbatim statutory quote exists in the provided analysis. Under the Grounding Rule this classifies as Locally Discretionary.

elements.element: Written parental consent for mental health care services to students under 18: no team-provided mental health service without prior written parental consent; consent on district-developed form complying with state/federal law; parent may consent to ongoing services or limit to single occasion
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §37.115 (current enacted text confirmed via web search)
elements.supporting_quote: A team may not provide a mental health care service to a student who is under 18 years of age unless the team obtains written consent from the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student before providing the mental health care service. The consent required by this subsection must be submitted on a form developed by the school district that complies with all applicable state and federal law. The student's parent or person standing in parental relation to the student may give consent for a student to receive ongoing services or may limit consent to one or more services provided on a single occasion.
elements.verification_method: web search confirmed — verbatim statutory text retrieved from texas.public.law/statutes/tex._educ._code_section_37.115
elements.local_additions: None — the policy tracks the statutory text closely.

elements.element: Record retention: materials and information from a student threat assessment must be maintained in the student's school record until the student's 24th birthday
elements.supporting_quote: Materials and information provided to or produced by a team during a threat assessment of a student under this section must be maintained in the student's school record until the student's 24th birthday.
elements.verification_method: verbatim statute text | web search confirmed
elements.local_additions: None — the policy language mirrors the statutory mandate verbatim.

elements.element: Superintendent shall coordinate with law enforcement and take appropriate action in accordance with the District's multihazard emergency operations plan
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §37.108 (cited in statute analysis but no verbatim quotes extracted)
elements.supporting_quote: None — the statute analysis for TEC §37.108 expressly states: 'Complete verbatim statutory text was not retrieved. No elements have been extracted because no verbatim supporting quotes are available from the provided source text.'
elements.local_additions: TEC §37.108 generally governs multihazard emergency operations plans, but because no verbatim supporting quote was available in the statute analysis, this element cannot be grounded as Required by Law or Permitted by Law. The district's decision to assign this coordination duty specifically to the Superintendent is also a local implementation choice.

elements.element: Terminology modernization: 'substance use prevention and intervention' (replacing 'substance abuse program')
elements.supporting_quote: A team identifying a student using or possessing tobacco, drugs, or alcohol shall act in accordance with district policies and procedures related to substance use prevention and intervention.
elements.local_additions: None — the updated terminology directly tracks the mandatory statutory language.

elements.element: Expanded statement of team purpose: 'to the community, school, or individual and support the District in implementing the District's multihazard emergency operations plan' (replacing 'to another person, the campus, or the community')
elements.verification_method: verbatim statute text — Grounding Rule applied; TEC §37.108 yields no verbatim supporting quotes in the statute analysis and TEC §37.115 does not contain this specific framing
elements.local_additions: The addition of language linking team purpose to the multihazard emergency operations plan is a local policy articulation without a grounded statutory basis in the provided analysis.

elements.element: Parental notification before threat assessments: notify parent before conducting assessment; provide opportunity to participate in person or remotely; provide opportunity to submit information to the team; provide findings and conclusions after completing assessment
elements.supporting_quote: Before a team may conduct a threat assessment of a student, the team must notify the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student regarding the assessment. | the team shall provide an opportunity for the parent or person to: (1) participate in the assessment, either in person or remotely | (2) submit to the team information regarding the student. | After completing a threat assessment of a student, the team shall provide to the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student the team's findings and conclusions regarding the student.
elements.verification_method: verbatim statute text | web search confirmed — H.B. 473 signed by governor, effective September 1, 2023; no injunction identified
elements.local_additions: None — the policy tracks all four statutory requirements without material addition.

compliance_summary: Parental Notification Before Assessments: Under H.B. 473 (2023), school districts are now required to notify a parent before conducting a threat assessment of their child. This policy reflects that legal requirement and adds that parents may participate in person or remotely and may submit information to the team.

Written Consent for Mental Health Services: State law under TEC §37.115 requires that a school district obtain written parental consent before providing mental health services to a student under 18. This policy follows that requirement and specifies that parents may consent to ongoing services or limit consent to a single occasion.

Special Education Protections: H.B. 6 (2025) added a requirement to TEC §37.115 that when a threat assessment involves a special education student, at least one team member must have specific knowledge of that student's disability. The law also allows — but does not require — the team to include an educational diagnostician or behavior specialist.

Record Retention Requirement: TEC §37.115 requires that materials from a student threat assessment be kept in the student's school record until the student turns 24. This policy mirrors that statutory language directly.
Terminology Update: TEC §37.115 uses the phrase 'substance use prevention and intervention,' and this policy updates its language to match, replacing the older term 'substance abuse program.'

Staff Threat Notification: This policy adds a locally created requirement that administrators or the threat assessment team notify affected teachers and aides about a threat as soon as it is safe and practical to do so. This specific staff notification process is not required by state law and reflects a choice made by the district.

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's FFB (Student Welfare: Crisis Intervention) policy adds specificity to the qualifications required for threat assessment team members, allows a single team to serve multiple campuses under defined conditions, and introduces new requirements for threat assessments involving students in special education programs, including the mandatory inclusion of at least one qualified special education professional on the assessment team.
changes_added: who have expertise in counseling, behavior management, mental health and substance use, classroom instruction, special education, school administration, school safety and security, emergency management, and law enforcement
A team may serve more than one campus of a school district, provided that:
Each district campus is assigned a team; and
In serving a particular campus, the team includes the person designated to serve as the campus behavior coordinator for that campus.

If a student in a special education program, as defined by law, is the subject of a threat assessment, the team conducting the threat assessment must include at least one of the following persons who has specific knowledge of the student's disability and the disability's manifestations:
A special education teacher who provides instruction to the student;
A Licensed Behavior Analyst;
An educational diagnostician;
A behavior specialist;
A Licensed Clinical Social Worker or a Licensed Master Social Worker; or
A Licensed School Psychologist.
timeline_local.notes: The cover page does not contain a large bold heading indicating a specific board action (e.g., "Discuss Revisions," "Adopt Revisions," etc.) — it only shows the policy title and metadata. Action has been marked as 'unknown' because the action type cannot be determined from the available cover page content. All red underlined text has been captured; no red strikethrough text was identified in the document.

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's FFB (Local) Crisis Intervention policy strengthens the threat assessment process by requiring team member expertise in specific areas, mandating parental notification and participation before and after assessments, and clarifying protocols for students at risk of suicide who also make threats of violence. It also updates substance-related language from "having a substance abuse issue" to "using or possessing tobacco, drugs, or alcohol."
changes_added: The Superintendent of the district shall ensure, to the greatest extent practicable, that the members appointed to each team have expertise in counseling, behavior management, mental health and substance use, classroom instruction, special education, school administration, school safety and security, emergency management, and law enforcement.

Before an assessment takes place: a. Notifying the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student regarding the assessment. b. Providing an opportunity for the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student to participate in the assessment, either in person or remotely. c. Providing an opportunity for the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student to submit to the team information regarding the student.
After completing a threat assessment of a student, providing to the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student the team's findings and conclusions regarding the student.

If the student at risk of suicide also makes a threat of violence to others, the team shall conduct a threat assessment in addition to actions taken in accordance with the district's suicide prevention program.
using or possessing tobacco, drugs, or alcohol

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's FFB (Local) Student Welfare: Crisis Intervention policy strengthens the threat assessment process by requiring the Superintendent to ensure team members have specific expertise, mandating parental notification and participation opportunities before and after assessments, and clarifying procedures for students at risk of suicide who also threaten others. It also updates substance-related language from "having a substance abuse issue" to "using or possessing tobacco, drugs, or alcohol."
changes_added: The Superintendent of the district shall ensure, to the greatest extent practicable, that the members appointed to each team have expertise in counseling, behavior management, mental health and substance use, classroom instruction, special education, school administration, school safety and security, emergency management, and law enforcement.

Providing an opportunity for the parent of or person standing in parental relation to the student to submit to the team information regarding the student.

If the student at risk of suicide also makes a threat of violence to others, the team shall conduct a threat assessment in addition to actions taken in accordance with the district's suicide prevention program.
